snippets.title: What is Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)?
snippets.content: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) is a technique that enhances large language model outputs by retrieving relevant documents from a knowledge corpus before generating a response. Instead of relying solely on parametric knowledge baked into model weights, RAG fetches grounding context at inference time. This reduces hallucination and keeps answers up-to-date without retraining the model.

snippets.title: TF-IDF: Term Frequency–Inverse Document Frequency
snippets.content: TF-IDF is a classical information retrieval scoring method. Term Frequency (TF) measures how often a word appears in a document; Inverse Document Frequency (IDF) down-weights words that appear in many documents (e.g., 'the', 'is'). The product TF-IDF highlights words that are distinctive to a specific document. Unlike neural embeddings, TF-IDF is deterministic, fast, and requires no model download.

snippets.title: Cosine Similarity vs Dot Product
snippets.content: Cosine similarity measures the angle between two vectors, normalizing for magnitude. It is the standard metric for semantic similarity because it is insensitive to document length. Dot product is magnitude-sensitive: longer documents with more term overlap score higher regardless of relevance. For normalized embeddings cosine and dot product are equivalent; for unnormalized TF-IDF vectors, cosine is generally preferred.

snippets.title: How RAG differs from fine-tuning
snippets.content: Fine-tuning updates model weights by training on new data, which is expensive and can cause catastrophic forgetting. RAG, by contrast, keeps the model frozen and instead retrieves relevant context from an external store. Fine-tuning is better for adapting style or domain-specific reasoning; RAG is better for injecting frequently-changing factual knowledge.

snippets.title: The Transformer Architecture
snippets.content: Transformers use a self-attention mechanism to process all tokens in a sequence in parallel, capturing long-range dependencies more effectively than RNNs. The key components are multi-head attention layers, feed-forward networks, and positional encodings. BERT and GPT are both transformer-based but differ in training objective: BERT is bidirectional (masked language modeling) while GPT is autoregressive (next-token prediction).

answer: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) is a technique that improves large language model responses by first retrieving relevant documents from a knowledge corpus and then generating an answer using that retrieved context. Instead of relying only on knowledge stored in the model’s weights, RAG fetches grounding information at inference time, which helps reduce hallucinations and keeps answers up to date without retraining the model.
